rewards.title: Happy birthday
rewards.body:
As I pedaled as usual, I suddenly realized something.
Come to think of it, when was Shimamura's birthday?
As if I had remembered something very important that I had forgotten, I turned around immediately. Of course, on the way home there was no trace of Shimamura. I had realized it, but I didn't know what to do and it was eating away at me inside.
I stopped and thought for a moment.
Then I put one foot down, jumped like a grasshopper, and turned the bicycle in the other direction.
If I left it for tomorrow, I was sure I wouldn't be able to sleep at night, so I decided to go congratulate her right away.
As I headed to Shimamura's house, the sun had half hidden and night was beginning to awaken. The air I breathed in the darkness was so cold it seemed to imitate winter. Winter was coming.
What worried me most now was whether Shimamura would even come out from under the covers.
I arrived at her house. My eyes went to the light escaping through the window. As I watched it, I took out my phone.
"Hello, Shimamura?"
As I spoke, I thought about why I was calling her when there was a doorbell.
«Well, yes?»
"I'm in front of your house."
«Huh? What's up? Well, never mind, wait a moment.»
"Okay."
I hung up. I stood still for a moment, holding the phone against my chest, and then looked up to wait for Shimamura.
"Adachi, you really do appear out of nowhere."
Shimamura came out. She was no longer in uniform. And two other people came with her: Shimamura's little sister and that girl with the slightly weird hair color. All three were crunching on the same fried rice crackers. Their posture was almost identical, and seeing them like that, even the girl with different hair seemed to be her sister. I was disconcerted by the unexpected number of people.
"G-good evening."
"Yes, good evening. And we were just walking home together a moment ago."
"You're so weird," said Shimamura as she continued nibbling on her cracker. Weird? I'm always told that.
Still feeling intimidated, I told her why I had come.
"Can I, um... celebrate your birthday now?"
"Huh?"
Shimamura opened her eyes wide, as if wondering what I was talking about so suddenly. It was an expression of hers I often saw.
I guess I'm usually too impulsive. I wish I could act more logically.
"It's just... I forgot your birthday this year."
"Oh, right. My birthday is in April. I had forgotten too."
"Huh?"
"What are you talking about? I did congratulate you."
Shimamura's sister tugged on her sister's shirt. "Oh, really?", Shimamura tilted her head, and the little one puffed out her cheeks. "Oh, yes, yes, of course it happened," Shimamura tried to fix it. "Liar," the girl replied without giving her time, exposing her instantly. "Hehehe," Shimamura laughed to cover it up.
"It did happen, right?"
Shimamura looked for support from the strange-haired girl, but she replied with surprising calm: "I have no idea at all." Then, she offered me a fried rice cracker saying: "Have one." I saw her hugging the bag of sweets as if it were a treasure. Was she handing them out to everyone?
"Th-thanks."
When I accepted it, she gave me a big, very friendly smile... I didn't really know how to react.
I wasn't good with children. Or rather, I was aware that I wasn't good at dealing with people in general.
"So, what are we going to do to celebrate?"
Asked Shimamura after finishing her cracker and wiping her fingers.
"Uh..."
I had come on impulse, so I didn't even have a gift prepared. What do I do?
"H-happy birthday!"
"Thank you."
I nodded awkwardly, bowing several times. At least I had been able to congratulate her... I'd give her gift tomorrow.
"Is that all?"
"Y-yes."
"Then... if you have nothing to do, do you want to stay for dinner? Though we'll have to wait for my dad to come back."
"Huh? Oh, yes... yes."
Eating with everyone really sounded exhausting, but I wanted to spend more time with Shimamura, so I ended up nodding without thinking.
"Then come in," she said, motioning with her hand.
Although I thought she was treating me like a dog, I followed her anyway.
As I took off my shoes at the entrance, Shimamura's little sister watched me with a slightly stern expression. Should I greet her?
"G-good evening."
When I greeted her stiffly, she tilted her head slightly and immediately ran off behind her sister.
...Neither of us was used to this.
In the living room, Shimamura's mother was relaxing watching TV and laughing heartily.
"So, it turns out today is my birthday."
"Oh, really? You were born, how amazing."
Shimamura's mother clapped with total disinterest. Then, seeing me waiting behind, her face softened.
"Oh, welcome."
The way she relaxed her expression was very similar to Shimamura's.
"Have you come to hang out at this hour?"
"She says she came because she forgot to wish me a happy birthday."
"What an interesting girl."
Shimamura's mother laughed carefreely, showing her teeth and shaking her shoulders. I, for my part, shrank in embarrassment upon hearing it.
Adults, it seems, instead of calling you weird, tried to be considerate.
"I see, so it's your birthday. Then I'll make you your favorite food."
"What is it, what is it?"
"Mom's homemade tamagoyaki."
"Of course, of course."
"Get ready to cry with emotion when you try it."
Shimamura's mother got up, and as soon as she left the spot, Shimamura sat down. Then her little sister quickly settled between Shimamura's legs.
Ah...
I almost let out a sound without meaning to.
Shimamura's sister looked up at me, narrowing her eyes. As if saying: It's normal, right?
"Uuu...", I groaned internally; since my rival was who she was, I couldn't say anything and docilely sat down next to Shimamura.
"Then I'll get on Shimamura's head."
Climb, climb, climb... the strange-haired girl skillfully climbed from her shoulders to her head. Her head? While I doubted it, she really settled right on top. She held on and sat there quietly.
"Isn't she heavy?"
"Curiously, I don't feel her weight."
A strand of her pale blue hair fell, adorning Shimamura. An almost luminous glow bathed her cheeks.
"More than anything, she's warm."
"They're all stuck to me," said Shimamura with an awkward smile.
"Happy birthday, Shimamura."
The strange girl congratulated her while waving her arms above her head. Shimamura looked up and smiled softly.
"Thank you."
"Will there be a feast today?"
"You like tamagoyaki, don't you?"
"Waa!"
The strange-haired girl raised her arms excitedly. For some reason, she didn't slip off her head despite having let go.
"Well, now it's your turn."
Shimamura poked her little sister's back.
The girl turned around pouting.
"I think forcing people to do this kind of thing is in very poor taste."
"It's fashionable."
Her answer made no sense.
"I said it before."
"It's okay to repeat it."
Shimamura took her sister's tied hair and started moving it back and forth, playing with it.
"Graaah!", the little one growled.
Seeming satisfied with the reaction, Shimamura laughed innocently.
Sometimes, when witnessing that childish side of her, I feel something as intense as dizziness swirling inside me.
Without warning, Shimamura brought her face close to mine. Her hair brushed my face like a caress, and my eyes began to wander, nervous.
My heart was already racing as it was.
"Maybe Adachi doesn't like it much... but I don't dislike this atmosphere."
"Huh? Ah, no, it's not that..."
Before I could excuse myself by saying it didn't bother me, Shimamura looked me straight in the eyes.
"If possible, I'd like you to accept it."
As she said that, she pulled me toward her, hugging my head, and my thoughts scattered at once.
Wrapped in something soft and fluffy, I ended up nodding as if hypnotized.

rewards.title: The Noes of Elephant
rewards.body:
"I haven't been to the aquarium in ages, but it was actually a lot of fun. That place has really changed over the years."
"Ah."
"If it were closer, I'd go all the time."
"Hmm..."
"Yeah. It was really fun."
"Unlike talking to you."
"Why do you say that?"
"Because you don't listen to a single word I say."
"Of course I do. By the way, did you know I can't swim?"
"I'm pretty sure we met at the pool."
"Whatever. Anyway, I was thinking of going to the zoo next time."
"Oh, yeah."
"You should come too!"
"Huh?"


"It'll be fun!"
"Shut up."
I wasn't really sure why, but I had ended up letting that woman drag me along with her again. There was definitely some kind of magic in her irritating antics and that way of speaking that bordered on exasperating. If you filled her with merchandise or something and put her out to sell it, you could probably make quite a bit of money.
Not that it was any of my business, I thought to myself as I headed to her house. There, next to the car, a group of people was waiting for me. There was her — obviously — her husband, her youngest daughter, and then... someone else.
She saw me right away and immediately started with her usual nonsense. As for her husband, he greeted me in a much more normal way.
"Good morning."
"Good morning. Sorry... I don't want to intrude on your family outing."
"No, no. It's fine. The more the merrier."
"Oh? Oh? Who is this? Obviously I know, but who is this?"
We both ignored her.
"But seriously, don't worry about it. Besides, you're not the only guest we have."
Her husband wrapped his arms around the girl and lifted her up, almost as if he were showing me the family cat or something. Coincidence or not, the girl was wearing overalls — or maybe pajamas — with an animal theme, so it wasn't such a far-fetched comparison. I watched as her little limbs dangled in the air.
It wasn't the first time I'd wondered, but... who exactly was she? And why was her hair so... blue?
"And what are you supposed to be today?"
"A tiger."
An attempted growl came from her tiny mouth. That didn't sound like any tiger I'd ever heard in my life.
"To me, you look more like a very colorful cat."
With that, her husband released the cat — ahem, tiger cub — only for his daughter to catch her in midair. Then they started spinning around in circles laughing. To be honest, they seemed to be having quite a good time.
"Kids, right? Hahaha."
The woman seemed very proud of herself. Okay...?
"She's clearly not yours. Why is she always hanging around your house?"
"Oh, right. She's an alien."
It sounded like she had just found her third lost child. Really? I looked at her husband expecting some additional comment, but he just nodded.
"Seems like it."
He sounded like he could barely remember.
"...And what's an alien doing in your house?"
"My eldest daughter brought her. And since she's a pretty nice kid, she lives with us."
"That's not how things work. You really should... rethink all of this."
"No, no. It's fine. She follows me when I go shopping and tells me all kinds of things about space. It's very interesting talking to her."
There she went again, completely ignoring me. Aliens? Really? Although, seeing the little creature playing in front of me, I couldn't deny that she had a kind of... interplanetary aura.
"Lucky she happens to look like a human, right? It would be hard to communicate with her if she looked more like, I don't know, a fish."
"...Personally, I feel like you wouldn't have any trouble communicating with a fish."
It was painfully easy for me to imagine the scene of her excitedly listening to the life story of a fish swimming in a small fishbowl while nodding repeatedly.
But anyway, enough of that. We had a zoo to visit. How many years had it been since the last time? I hoped they'd at least gotten some funnier animals.
Sensing that I was ready to go, the woman raised her hand and opened the back seat door.
"I'm sitting in the back!"
"Oh? I thought you'd take the passenger seat."
"Well, no. You don't want to mess with the driver. You could die."
"Or you could just... not? Have you never thought of that?"
"That would be boring."
Refusing to comment further, the woman got into the vehicle. I assumed that left the passenger seat for me. Honestly, I didn't mind. It was much better than having to sit next to her, that's for sure.
When was the last time I sat in the front seat? When was the last time I was in a car? It seemed like I was breaking a lot of streaks today.
With all kinds of thoughts flowing through my mind, I turned toward the windshield and... nothing. No trace of sentimentality. Nothing at all.
Behind me, the alien girl — or tiger cub, if you prefer — had been placed in the middle seat, between the woman on one side and her daughter on the other. I caught a glimpse of the daughter putting a small candy in her mouth.
"Just one, okay?", I think she said.
The girl's cheeks puffed up instantly, to the point where yes, it was practically impossible to see her as a tiger and not a cat.
"Give me one too."
"Just one, okay?"
The woman ate the candy with the same enthusiasm.
Looking at those two, I ended up locking eyes with the tiger cub.
"You're Adachi's mommy."
"Eh... yes?"
Not only did she have blue hair; her eyes too, shining like a pair of gems. Even her nails and teeth had a slight bluish tinge.
Honestly, I was starting to believe that whole alien thing. It certainly sounded more plausible than... any other alternative.
"She's a bit... different, isn't she?"
I hope you can forgive me for not finding the right words right now. It's not like I expected to be talking to an alien on the way to the zoo.
"Me?"
"Yes? It's not like there are any other weirdos here."
"Well, there is one..."
"He's calling me a weirdo, Dad."
"My own daughter? A weirdo? I'm going to cry," the father replied.
"How cruel..."
Waves of fake crying came at me from all directions.
"What's with this family...?"
"Wahaha."
"Hahaha."
"Hehehe."
Truth be told, the woman wasn't the only strange one there. They all shared that title.


The zoo we went to was located outside the city. It was absolutely enormous and even had a garden full of all kinds of exotic plants.
Though nowhere near as grand, I also remembered visiting a zoo once on a family trip.
As the smell of animals and vegetation filled the vehicle, those memories came back to me.
Back then, my husband still lived with me. And my daughter too.
These days, I was the only one in that house. Not that I minded. In any case, I felt that living alone suited my personality better. I had been through all that, built a family, and then, as if someone had flipped a switch, lost it all.
Did I regret anything? No, honestly I didn't.
That would have required a certain amount of happy memories... memories I lacked.
We started walking together through the zoo. I didn't know if it was real or just my mind playing tricks on me, but it seemed to me that the variety of animals there was much more diverse than it had been in that other zoo so many years ago. They had tanukis, serows, pelicans...
Honestly, how did they manage to take care of them all? Working there must have been a nightmare. I, for my part, had barely been able to raise my own daughter.
Finally, we reached the lion enclosure. I watched as the creature majestically devoured a piece of raw meat before turning toward our tiger cub, who was currently sucking on a candy. I wondered how the animals would see that mysterious alien. Also, who would win in a fight: an alien or a lion? I know, I know. I was too old to be thinking about things like that.
Next, we reached the aviary. Screaming louder than any bird could, the woman ran right in with the girls. Since I didn't feel like following her, I decided to stay outside this time.
While I waited, I felt my throat starting to dry up. I immediately started looking for a vending machine or something, but then the woman's husband appeared from a corner. He gave me a small nod and I returned it before taking the bottle of green tea he was offering me.
"I'm sorry about that. She just gets too excited when she sees animals."
"Some people never grow up, huh?", I commented jokingly, making the man laugh.
"You said it. She's a very restless person, that's for sure. She always has to be doing something. She was like that when we met too. I thought with time she'd calm down, but if anything, she's gotten worse."
It was quite a peculiar way for a husband to talk about his wife. On the other hand, as someone who'd had the misfortune of knowing her for over a decade, I definitely understood what he meant. The woman had mastered the art of being irritating, always backing off just when her victim was about to lose their temper.
"She's made quite a few friends with her attitude, but also a not-insignificant number of enemies. And yet, no matter how many times it backfires on her, she just keeps going. Personally, I think that's quite admirable."
"Hmm... Maybe."
Being able to act so friendly with people you'd just met had to be some kind of gift, there was no other way to see it.
I, for my part, still hadn't decided whether I liked that or not.
It wasn't long before the woman, her daughter, and the alien returned from the aviary. All three had huge smiles on their faces, a sign that they'd really enjoyed the experience. The man moved away from my side to go take care of the girls, and as if taking his place, the woman walked toward me.
"What were you two talking about?"
"You."
"Really? You're going to make me blush."
I looked in the direction of the woman's husband, only to find him lifting the blue-haired girl in the air and asking his daughter if they "looked like a giraffe." What? Well, never mind. I didn't want to know.
"That man is such an airhead. My eldest daughter takes after him quite a bit."
"Your eldest daughter? Oh, right..."
She was referring to the one who lived with my daughter... the person she wanted to spend the rest of her life with.
"And you? Are you having fun?"
"It's okay."
"Come here, let me lift you up too!"
The woman extended her arms as if she were completely serious. I hit her on the head and sighed.
"I remembered the last time I visited a zoo. That was decades ago."
"Was your daughter with you?"
"Yes, and my husband too. Hmm... I think there was a stuffed animal or something that she wanted. I don't know why, but she never asked me to buy it for her. Although now I do wish I had. I feel like things might have been different..."
Maybe I should have taken the initiative. Maybe I should have shown her my affection. Personally, I always hated when people did that, and I instinctively assumed she hated it too. And yet, I was wrong; even though we were mother and daughter, we were hardly the same person.
For some reason, the woman decided the next reaction was appropriate:
"Well, in that case, why don't you buy her a stuffed animal now and give it to her?"
"...She's an adult. Not a child."
"So? What difference does it make if you do it now?"
God, how much could one person annoy?
"I loved gifts when I was little and I still love them now! Give me something!"
"Shut up."
"Whether it's a giraffe or an elephant, I love them all!"
"What am I going to do with you...?"
"Although tigers do scare me a little. They're nothing like our adorable little tiger. No, they have killer eyes."
"...Well, yes. That's what tigers do. They kill."
This conversation was going nowhere. I felt myself getting more irritated by the second. It didn't help that the woman was practically shouting.
There wasn't a single part of her that I thought I could get along with. And yet, together, somehow, all those parts managed to touch my heart.
Brighter even than the sun, her genuine honesty shone through my cold exterior. It was as if I were being born again.
So... that happened.
We passed by the gift shop on the way back. Remembering my daughter's tearful face from so many years ago, I ended up buying an elephant stuffed animal.
I picked it up and, behind its head, I saw Yoshika wrestling with a giant bear.

rewards.title: Shimamura's profile unlocked!
rewards.body: Shimamura Hougetsu's profile card is now available in the sidebar menu.

rewards.title: Last year we celebrated Christmas, how about we do something different this winter break?
rewards.body:
If only we had hobbies in common. Then I'd have something to write to her about.
I tried to think of something, anything, but absolutely nothing came to mind.
Two full days had already passed since I decided to write to Shimamura. Since then, I'd spent hours and hours trying to find a topic that was interesting and worth it — and, more importantly, that wouldn't make me look like a weirdo — that we could talk about, but all my efforts had been in vain. It was quite strange. Even though I hadn't seen her during all of winter break, I found it impossible to think of any particular topic I wanted to talk to her about.
What did Shimamura like? What were her hobbies? I buried my head in my pillow as I tried to find an answer. Of course, I wouldn't have any of these problems if I knew. Maybe we could talk about New Year's. After all, it had just passed. Although, come to think of it, I doubted the conversation would go much beyond how much money our parents had given us. Speaking of my parents, I was starting to worry they might knock on my door soon if I kept spending so much time thinking about this. Of course, they usually let me do what I wanted, but even they had their limits. With that in mind, I took out my phone and forced myself to write the following message.
"Has anything interesting happened?"
What a boring message, even for me. I was basically leaving the ball in Shimamura's court, although at this point, what other choice did I have? With the message already sent, I slowly placed my phone on my pillow before getting under the covers, including my head. If a watched pot never boils, I imagined the same must apply to phones.
The trick seemed to work, and soon the device rang. I immediately came out from under the covers, grabbed my phone, and opened the message.
"Not really. Happy face."
That was it. What? "Happy face"?
Had she tried to write an actual smiley face, but her phone's autocorrect had messed it up and she couldn't be bothered to go back and fix it? Given her personality, that certainly seemed like a real possibility. It fit perfectly with the perception I had of her.
"Hmm... My perception of her..."
Although that message perfectly encapsulated Shimamura in my mind, I ended up wondering how she saw me. More precisely, what was her perception of me? And no, before you jump to conclusions, I didn't mean it in any weird way.
Wondering why I felt the need to make excuses like that, I decided to ask her directly.
"Hey, Shimamura. What do you think of me?"
I was silent for a few moments before finally pressing the "send" button.
I wonder, would I have done that with anyone else? Or was it specifically because I was talking to Shimamura that I decided to think twice? Before I could find the answer to those questions, my phone rang once more. I took the device and brought it closer to me.
A wave of expectation and uncertainty ran through my mind as I opened the message.
"🥺"
"Huh? Wh-What?"
An emoji? What was that supposed to mean? I quickly sent her another message to clarify.
As before, I didn't end up having to wait long for her response.
"That's the face you make when you come running out crying."
"...What? I-I don't think so..."
I repeatedly shook my head at the screen as if she could see me. Naturally, she couldn't. What did happen, however, was that I received a new message.
"Here's a better picture."
This one came with an attachment. It was a drawing of a caricatured version of me running with tears streaming down my cheeks and my hands raised. And yes, I was making that face. All while shouting "Shimamuraaa!".
The background was — for some reason — a grassy plain, with butterflies forming a circle around a giant swirling sun. In the middle of it all was me. Wait... That part looked very different from the rest. Had she drawn it with her sister?
I stared at the drawing. This was how Shimamura saw me. This was...
"...Nobita Nobi?"
Was she seeing me the same way Doraemon saw Nobita when he came running to him crying? Surely not. It couldn't be. No. Once again, I covered my head with the sheets in a desperate attempt to deny that conclusion.
As my skin brushed against the rough fabric, I could feel it starting to warm up a little.

The boomerang from before was still on my shelf. I took a quick look at it before setting Shimamura's drawing as my phone wallpaper. Was there anyone else in this whole world capable of doing something like that? Probably not. That thought crossed my mind like a gust of fresh wind, perfectly cooling my burning skin.

rewards.title: Maybe it wasn't planned that we'd end up like this in ten years
rewards.body:
The sky, the day of the week, and my mood aligned perfectly, like the overlapping hands of a clock. Just as I was getting ready to go fishing, the sliding door burst open. A small figure with vermilion sleeves fluttering ran in.
"Grandma!"
"At least don't get it wrong when you call me."
Being called aunt is one thing, but being called grandma is too much.
I have four older siblings, all with quite an age gap from me, and this was the fourth one's daughter. She's about five years old; that is, she's my niece. Like me when I was a child, despite being so small, they've dressed her in a full kimono. Although, since she's been running around, it's come quite loose.
"Aunty, play with me."
She clung to my sleeve. When she gets like this, it's hard to deal with her.
"Who are you calling aunty?"
"Dad told me to call you that."
"Huh?"
"He said just calling you Aunt wasn't any fun, so to make it sound cuter I should be nice and call you Aunty."
"That idiot of my brother."
As always, his sense of consideration is a bit off.
"Go have your father play with you."
"I don't really understand how Dad plays."
"Yeah, I get you, the truth is..."
For better or worse, my brother doesn't treat children like children. He treats them as equals, as adults.
For being the daughter of such a rigid brother — a man who seems to have been molded under pressure in the narrow space beneath geta sandals — she's quite bold. If anything, her temperament is more like mine. Maybe it's tradition among the Hino family children that the boys are born rigid and the girls are more relaxed.
"There's no one here but adults besides you, Aunty."
"Hey, exactly how do you see me, huh?"
I wish she'd decide once and for all whether I'm an aunt or a child.
"It's just that adults, when they're busy, won't play with you."
"I'm busy too, you know?"
Well, I understand how she feels. I was exactly the same. Even my closest-in-age brother already seemed almost like an adult to me when I was a child. That's why I let the maid take my hand to go play at that house. Her house, so small compared to ours, felt incredibly familiar and welcoming.
For now, it seemed my niece didn't have friends like that.
"Besides, this clothing is hard to run in," my niece complained, extending her sleeves.
"Get used to it. Once you do, you'll even be able to do backflips in that clothing."
Ooh, my niece's eyes sparkled.
"Hey, hey, teach me!"
"Your aunt, as you can see, is thinking about going fishing."
"Pleeease, pleeease, pleeease!"
"...Hey."
For some reason, I was being pulled by the sleeves from two different sides. I looked toward the side that wasn't my niece.
It was Nagafuji.
"When did you come in?"
"I was wandering around the front of the house, and when they saw my face, they let me in."
"Why do you always move like you're a suspicious person?"
"It's just that at the Hino house, there are many things to see in the garden."
"Oh, what big breasts."
My niece smiled innocently at Nagafuji.
Nagafuji was frozen solid.
"What's up, big breasts?"
"I wonder who taught you to say that..."
"My aunty."
"Hey, aunty."
"What's up, big breasts?"
It's not a lie, is it? See. I patted her on the chest and, in return, got a slap on the head.
Even after ten years, the basics don't change. Our height difference didn't shorten in the end either.
"Well, I guess it's okay."
"If it's okay, don't hit me..."
Nagafuji took off her glasses and stared at me.
"Hino likes big breasts."
"...Hey."
I guess... she's not wrong.
The things that have increased and the things that haven't changed. Both are here, in this room. And, honestly, the balance isn't bad at all.


 
Today we were in the middle of the "Adachi pillow performance test."
Lying on the sofa, I used Adachi's thigh as a pillow while watching TV. It was a formation with no gaps.
Adachi had let her hair grow longer than before and now wore glasses to watch TV.
"Shimamura, when you have free time, you fall asleep right away."
Her laugh, "ahaha," now sounded much more natural. She had matured quite a bit more than before.
Back then we were both rougher or, rather, it was like we gathered raw emotions just to break them afterwards. Sometimes they were spiky shapes, other times soft... That's how I remember it now.
"I once heard your mother say you loved to sleep."
"Well, maybe."
"She said you were a child who didn't cause problems because you spent all your time sleeping."
"Hmm..."
Was that really true? I guess if I spent my time sleeping, it's natural I don't remember any of that.
I half wandered off. My cheek sank against Adachi's soft legs, and it was pleasant.
The two of us living together in an apartment. Going almost everywhere together.
From morning to night, Adachi and Shimamura.
I won't say it was absolutely everything, but I think most of what we imagined ten years ago has come true.
"Adachi."
"Hm?"
"It feels like a dream."
I wonder if this floating feeling is simply due to the drowsiness of falling asleep.
"Yes."
For some reason, I felt quite satisfied with the feeling Adachi had put into her response.
"Ah, it's time for the weather forecast..."
I murmured lazily while still lying down, halfheartedly looking for the remote. I glanced around and saw it had rolled to the edge of the sofa and fallen off.
"Oh."
I tried to reach it with my hand.
"Let's see..."
Then I tried to reach it with my foot. I was in a bad position, and unless I split my leg in half, the angle just wasn't there.
"I give up on the weather."
"Is that okay?"
"Yes."
I nodded, leaning my weight even more onto Adachi's legs.
"If it rains, we'll stay home and hang out. If it's sunny, we'll go out and have fun outside."
Because, no matter what happens, I'm sure it'll be a fun day.
I lay there and started to doze off. The TV screen blurred until I could no longer see it.
"Sounds good."
I heard Adachi's voice just before closing my eyes.
I had wanted to reply "yes, sounds good," but I don't know if it was heard.
That's why I decided I'd tell her again as soon as I woke up.

rewards.title: The mystery of the Shimamura family
rewards.body:
Lately there are three things that seem strange to me.
The first is that Hougetsu looks happy every day.
"What do you think, Mom?"
"About what?"
My wife, sitting on the floor with her back hunched while cutting her toenails, didn't seem to be paying even half attention to the conversation.
"If she's having fun, what's wrong with that?"
"Yes, that's true."
During the New Year's holidays, Hougetsu was usually always sleepy, or rather she spent her time sleeping. However, this year I've seen her moving around quite a bit. Seeing her profile with a smile painted on her face as she walked down the hallway, I couldn't help but have this little doubt.
Since she entered high school, she seems to have regained that calm atmosphere she had in elementary school. During her middle school years she had a pretty complicated rebellious phase, so I became a bit worried about her.
"Well, I'm always having fun, you know?"
"I see. I envy you."
It seemed my wife was in one of those moments when she turns off her brain. Just when I was about to give up, it was she who continued the conversation.
"Could it be that she got a boyfriend?"
"Boyfriend... I see."
I thought that could be an option. Although... really?
"Or maybe a girlfriend."
"...I see?"
What exactly did that mean? I tilted my head confused while my wife let out an unpleasant "ke-ke-ke" laugh.
"That Adachi looks a bit suspicious."
"Suspicious?"
"Yes, quite suspicious."
"Adachi... the girl from the other day?"
"Yes, yes. The quiet one."
That girl with Hougetsu... Hmm. Hmm?
"It's a complicated world."
"Well, who knows. Done! With this the mystery has been solved."
My wife closed the matter with a pretty vague conclusion. For my part, I looked around the room and into the distance letting out an "ah" and an "oh," feeling a bit uneasy. But after a while, I thought, well, it doesn't matter. I'm not as carefree as my wife, but I guess if my daughter is having fun, then everything's fine.
Now, the second thing. That girl my wife has been carrying under her arm for a good while.
"I get the impression she's glowing too much."
"She sure does glow, huh?"
"I'm radiant!," the glowing girl replied with total calm. She's a mysterious creature that, without me noticing, is always around.
"I can glow even more if you want."
"Don't, you'll blind us."
Since my wife kept carrying her, it seemed really uncomfortable to cut her nails.
"It's just that if I don't hold her, she'll immediately open the refrigerator."
"The kitchen is so much fuuun!"
The glowing girl's arms and legs shook with tremendous energy.
"Why do you never look inside the refrigerator?"
"Because if you open it, the food goes bad!"
"But... why does she glow?"
"I have no idea, but sometimes it's useful. Since she glows even in the middle of the night, you can see your surroundings."
"Pikaaa~"
Accompanying her lazy voice, it seemed... that she glowed even brighter.
However, this light blue glow reminded me of something I'd seen somewhere before... or maybe not.
"Just think of her as a talking cat that came to live here."
"That sounds good, but is it okay for her to stay?"
I like cats, but she's someone else's daughter. Won't her parents say anything?
"At least she's not a villain."
"You think?"
"Our daughters trust her."
My wife said that with total naturalness. She's that kind of person.
"Then, I guess there's nothing to worry about."
"Nope."
"Ah, there's Yachii."
As soon as our daughter poked her head into the room, my wife threw the glowing girl toward her.
"Here, take her."
The girl flew after the throw... Was she floating in the air? She did a slow loop around my daughter's head and then landed without making a sound. As she did, a trail of light blue particles scattered floating through the air.
I couldn't help it, I stood there with my mouth open, unable to say a word.
"Then, Shou, let's go to the kitchen right away!"
"We won't."
"Oh?"
My daughter took the glowing girl's hand and both ran out immediately.
"...They seem to be having fun over there too."
"Right? That's good."
"Well, I guess so."
"And I'm having fun too, heh, heh!"
"That's enough of that."
"Of course it's not enough," my wife replied with a loud laugh. Her way of laughing had always been peculiar, and deep down I was grateful the girls hadn't inherited that.
"And the third?"
"Huh?"
"You said there were three strange things."
My wife began to cut the nails on her left hand, which was now free.
"What a surprise, you were actually listening to me."
"I've always been told I'm a woman full of surprises."
I think that refers to something else. Probably to the fact that you usually appear out of nowhere as if you sprouted from a thick bamboo grove, or something like that.
"Come on, make your last wish."
"That wasn't the intention... Actually, I meant that you also look happy."
"I already told you I'm having fun."
"Do you want me to repeat it?," my wife asked, turning around with a smile.
"No, it's just that I thought you seemed more animated than usual."
"Oh, really? I don't notice it much myself."
She looked happy, even about things she didn't seem to fully understand.
My wife, who's always a bit strange, continued cutting her nails while humming a tune.

rewards.title: Shortly after
rewards.body:
My initial reaction when my daughter brought her fiancee home was one of astonishment.
"......"
That astonishment tripled once I heard she was a former student who had just graduated from high school.
By the time I realized she was a girl, I didn't even know what to say anymore.
"Nice to meet you! I'm Shimamura! Shimamura Hougetsu! I hope we get along, mother!"
What a noisy girl. We'd barely sat down for a moment and she was already calling me "mother." Although I had hoped that someday my daughter would get married and leave home, she had taken so long that I had started to wonder if she ever would, which was part of the reason this all took me so by surprise.
"You... are you going to marry her?"
I looked at my daughter as I asked this. Her fiancee's eyes sparkled, almost as if she was having trouble restraining herself from answering for her. Her expression was one of innocence, without showing any sign of fear of any kind. My daughter looked at her, then at me, and nodded.
"Yes..."
"Hmm? Adacchi, cheer up! Eisa!"
"Well, it's just... I didn't think we'd literally do this on the day you graduated."
I need some time to prepare, she seemed to want to add with her facial expression. Ah, I guess that explained why the girl was wearing her school uniform.
"Oh, please. I've been waiting for this day for a very long time."
"I wish I had known that too..."
A slight smile appeared on her face, showing that she didn't say it in a completely negative way.
Well, in that case.
"If you both want to do it... then I guess it's fine."
For my part, I had no reason to oppose this. I was just a little surprised that she was going to marry a former student, that's all.
The girl did look much younger than her, but again, if they both wanted this, I guess it was fine. Probably.
"Did you hear that, Adacchi? She said it's fine!"
"Umm, could you call me Miss Adachi, at least for this once?"
"I'll bring my parents next time! My mother especially is a bit... very... slightly lively!"
"Of course..."
I couldn't help but have a bad feeling; I had never heard her sound so evasive about anything before. What did she mean by "slightly lively"?
"Well, then it's decided. I'll quickly stop by my house and grab a futon and a change of clothes."
"Huh?"
Was that exclamation mine or my daughter's? It was impossible to tell.
"Are you going to live here?"
"For the time being! Ah, but don't worry! I'll visit my parents often!"
With that, the girl ran out of the living room. She was as cheerful as all the spring in the world combined. I could hear her rummaging in the entrance and finally slamming the door shut behind her.
Her youth was almost dazzling.
Sitting there, just the two of us now, my eyes met my daughter's.
"Technically she is my former student, yes, but that was when I used to teach elementary school..."
"I see..."
"I know how the age difference looks, but if you consider that..."
As my daughter tried to explain herself as best she could, a thought came to me: was this what all parents felt when their children got married? I had expected to feel a little more melancholic, but instead, I was met with a gust of wind that threatened to sweep me away with it into a clear sky.
Still, we were talking about my daughter.
"Congratulations on your marriage."
"Thank you..."
Those words were probably all that was required of me.
Trying to process the verbal bouquet I had thrown her, she tilted her head awkwardly.
A moment later, she smiled.


The next day, I was introduced to the girl's parents, something I would soon regret, but that's a story for another time.

rewards.title: Adachi and Shimamura chats
rewards.body:
When I returned to the room, there was one more seal.
I already had a pretty good idea who it was, so I compared both from above, wondering which was which, and picked one up.
It was the usual seal. Hello.
"Hehehe... You've fallen into the trap."
The seal left on the floor stood up on two legs with total naturalness. I thought so.
The face under the hood was still exactly my seal's.
"Hello, Shimamura."
"Yes, hello, alien."
"Today I tried to mimic a seal."
"Why?"
Compared to when she usually dresses up as a tanuki or a squirrel, at least this time she managed to blend into the house; in that sense, it was a surprisingly convincing disguise.
"Waa!"
"What a rarity!," she celebrated with both arms raised, overflowing with joy.
However, I had no idea what she gained from mimicking a seal. Maybe I should try using her as a pillow, just like I do with the original.
"Gyaaa!"
I put her on her side and tried to rest my head on her, but she was so extremely fluffy that she was actually uncomfortable. What the heck is this being made of? Touching her, it felt like the surface of the sea. Being able to rest your head on the surface of the water was, without a doubt, a very strange sensation. Oh well, never mind.
"It's almost lunchtime, so let's go, seal number two."
"Yeees!"
I waved goodbye to the real seal, who stayed in the room, and headed to the kitchen accompanied by that strange creature.
It was a very everyday scene in our home.

rewards.title: Adachi and Shimamura chats 2
rewards.body:
My grandmother, who lives in the countryside, sends me photos of Gon from time to time. Every time I see them, feelings of affection, relief that he's okay, and a kind of wearing melancholy mix within me. The final destination of those emotions usually ended up in my eyes and nose.
I hear the sound of a drop falling on the surface of the water that has formed in my heart.
As if trying to drown that feeling, a cheerful noise began to echo from the hallway. When I looked, I saw a hippopotamus walking briskly while shaking some maracas. She seemed to have really liked them, because lately I often saw her going back and forth with a maraca in each hand.
"Hey, you, the one who looks like she has nothing to do."
She stopped dead. After thinking "Hmm...", she decided it wasn't about her and cheerfully resumed the rhythmic shaka shaka of her maracas.
"I wasn't wrong, I'm talking to you."
"Oh!," she exclaimed, hopping on one leg before coming closer to me.
"I didn't think you meant me."
"And I'm surprised you're surprised. But you look very full of energy."
"It's because it's the season when food tastes better."
"Is there any season when you don't have an appetite?"
"Actually no, but..."
That she's in good health is excellent. As I contemplated the hippopotamus with shiny cheeks, something occurred to me.
"Make a pose."
The hippopotamus reflected again with a "Hmm..." and, in the end, extended both arms with a "Yaa!," like an anteater trying to intimidate someone.
"Well, this is enough," I thought, so I took a photo and sent it to my grandmother.
The reply came immediately.
«What is this cheerful creature?!»
The way she reacted made me feel the blood connection I shared with my mother.
«This summer we'll come visit you together.»
This year I won't leave her behind; I'll take her with me.
I realized that although it seems like nothing changes, there are small things that do.
Without thinking much, I stroked the head of the hippopotamus, who remained firm in her threatening pose.

rewards.title: Adachi's profile unlocked!
rewards.body: Adachi Sakura's profile card is now available in the sidebar menu.
